nodes.label: PE
nodes.description: High-density polyethylene is widely used for aquaculture netting because it offers low water absorption, chemical resistance, and a favorable strength-to-weight ratio.

references.title: FAO technical guidelines for aquaculture gear and materials

nodes.label: PA / Nylon
nodes.description: Polyamide fibers are valued for high tensile strength and abrasion resistance, but they absorb more water than polyethylene and require careful design for long deployments.

nodes.label: PET
nodes.description: Polyester can provide dimensional stability, UV resistance, and low creep in selected aquaculture net constructions.

references.title: Polyester fibers in marine textile applications

nodes.label: Monofilament
nodes.description: Single continuous filaments create smooth surfaces that can reduce drag and may make fouling organisms less able to attach.

nodes.label: Multifilament
nodes.description: Bundles of fine filaments improve flexibility and knot handling while increasing surface area compared with monofilament designs.

nodes.label: Twisted Yarn
nodes.description: Twisted yarn organizes fibers into a stable helix that balances strength, flexibility, and familiar net-making processes.

nodes.label: Braided Yarn
nodes.description: Braided yarn routes interlace strands to improve handling stability and distribute mechanical loading over more contact points.

nodes.label: 8-Strand Twine
nodes.description: Eight-strand twine combines yarn bundles into a strong aquaculture twine architecture used in high-load cage nets.

nodes.label: Braided Twine
nodes.description: Braided twine creates a compact construction with good dimensional stability and abrasion performance.

nodes.label: Knotless Net
nodes.description: Knotless netting removes knot stress concentrations, creating smoother mesh intersections and lowering fish abrasion risk.

nodes.label: Knotted Net
nodes.description: Knotted nets are established, repairable, and mechanically familiar, but knots can increase drag and local wear.

nodes.label: Semi-Rigid Net
nodes.description: Semi-rigid net designs use stiffer materials or structures to maintain cage volume and resist deformation under currents.

nodes.label: Copper Coating
nodes.description: Copper-based antifouling treatments suppress biofouling but require environmental risk assessment and regulatory compliance.

nodes.label: Silicone Release
nodes.description: Foul-release silicone surfaces reduce organism adhesion so cleaning forces can remove biofouling more easily.

nodes.label: UV Stabilizer
nodes.description: UV stabilizer packages slow polymer degradation in sunlight-exposed cage environments.

nodes.label: Low Fouling
nodes.description: Low fouling performance reduces cleaning frequency, maintains water exchange, and can improve fish welfare during long deployments.

nodes.label: High Strength
nodes.description: High strength net routes tolerate storm loading, currents, handling events, and higher exposed biomass systems.

nodes.label: Abrasion Resistance
nodes.description: Abrasion resistance helps nets survive contact with frames, service vessels, cleaning systems, and predator interactions.

nodes.label: Service Repairability
nodes.description: Service repairability captures how easily nets can be inspected, patched, cleaned, and redeployed during farm operations.

nodes.label: Offshore Salmon Farming
nodes.description: High-energy offshore salmon sites need robust nets with low fouling, strong twine routes, and predictable deformation behavior.

nodes.label: Nearshore Trout Farming
nodes.description: Nearshore trout farming benefits from maintainable nets that are easy to repair and suited to moderate exposure.

nodes.label: Predator-Resistant Enclosures
nodes.description: Predator-resistant enclosures emphasize abrasion tolerance, mesh stability, and resistance to biting or impact damage.
